text: Hello friends, this is a case of inguinal hernia in a female patient. This patient has right sided inguinal hernia and she has direct inguinal hernia and that is recurrent inguinal hernia.
She was already operated before but miss was not applied and it has recurred.
So initially the diagnosis was bilateral inguinal hernia. But once we entered inside then we saw that it was only direct right sided.
A small scar is visible on the right side of the patient's abdomen, indicating a previous surgical intervention.
The surgical team prepares to proceed with the hernia repair using laparoscopic techniques.
The surgeon introduces a Veress needle through the supraumbilical port, preparing for laparoscopic access.
The patient is positioned supine, and the surgeon uses an Ethicon Veress needle, which features a visible indicator that turns red when the tip is pressed and green when the tip is extended.
The surgeon uses a ball-shaped device to prevent gas leakage during the laparoscopic hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon performs the hanging drop test by introducing suction irrigation, causing the ball to float in the fluid, indicating a positive test result.

The surgeon inserts the Veress needle into the abdominal wall, ensuring a 90-degree angle with the wall and a 45-degree angle with the patient's body. The abdominal wall is lifted slightly, and a syringe with saline is used to perform an irrigation test, observing the fluid flow freely.

The surgeon performs an irrigation test followed by a suction test, observing bubbles as an indicator of a successful suction test. The hanging drop test is conducted by lifting the abdominal wall, allowing fluid to enter, confirming the test's success. Subsequently, the tubing of the insufflator is attached to begin creating pneumoperitoneum.

The surgeon uses a pressure setting of fifteen and a flow rate of one liter per minute to create pneumoperitoneum, with the actual pressure gradually increasing. Liver dullness is visibly obliterated as the abdominal cavity expands.
You will get a tympanic sound in the liver dullness area and that will be sound similar to the

The surgeon completes the creation of pneumoperitoneum and introduces the Veress needle. The incision is slightly enlarged, and the first trocar is inserted in the supraumbilical position for ipsilateral port placement.
The surgeon introduces the telescope through the suprapubic trocar, providing a clear view of the abdominal cavity for the hernia repair procedure.
The telescope is positioned within the surgical field, with white balancing and focusing completed to ensure clear visualization of the anatomical structures.
Gas is visibly released into the abdominal cavity, accompanied by a hissing sound, indicating the insufflation process during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon opens the valve, allowing gas to flow, which creates a visible transillumination effect in the surgical field.
The surgeon prepares to attach the light cable to a thirty-degree telescope, positioning it for optimal visualization during the hernia repair procedure.

The light from the telescope is visible percutaneously through the skin at the hernia site, where the muscle defect allows the hernia sac to push against the skin. Two epsilon ports are positioned according to the baseball diamond concept, revealing the hernia defect.
The surgeon uses an atraumatic grasper to pull the omentum out of the hernia sac, revealing the hernia's contents.
The surgeon observes that the hernia content is not adhered and is easily extracted. The direct hernia is visible, along with the inferior epigastric vessel in the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the medial umbilical ligament situated between the medial and lateral umbilical ligaments, confirming the presence of a direct right-sided inguinal hernia. The light cable is positioned to enhance visibility of the anatomical structures, including the median umbilical ligament in the midline, which is the obliterated urachus.

The surgeon observes the obliterated umbilical artery and lateral umbilical ligament, identifying the inferior epigastric vessel on the left side. The deep inguinal ring is visible with no hernia present on the left side. The bladder appears slightly filled, and the uterus is visible, showing no pathology but appearing bulky due to multiparity.

The surgeon identifies the ovary and fallopian tube, then pushes the small bowel upwards while tilting the patient's head 30 degrees down. The right ureter is visible with peristalsis. The sigmoid colon is gently pushed towards the left side for better visualization.

The surgeon visualizes the sacral promontory and median sacral vessels. The round ligament is seen finishing at the deep ring. The triangle of doom is identified, containing the external iliac artery and vein. The sigmoid colon is pushed slightly to the left, revealing the sacral promontory below.
The surgeon moves the peritoneum to reveal the sacral promontory, with the median sacral vessel visible in the middle of the surgical field.
The median sacral vessel is visible in the midline, with the ureter positioned at a noticeable distance from it.

The right ureter is visible, measuring approximately 2.5 centimeters, with noticeable peristalsis. The anterior longitudinal ligament is also visible, commonly used for sacral colpopexy or rectopexy. The anatomy of the sacral promontory and presacral area is clearly displayed.
After that you can just try to find out other structures.
The surgeon identifies the external cardiac vein, the cecum, and the omentum within the surgical field.
The tinea coli of the colon is visible, alongside the small intestine. Below, the base of the appendix is clearly observable.
The base of the appendix is visible, with the tip not visible as it extends retrocecal.
But we should not touch the tip. It's bad idea to hold the tip and stretch it and pull it because it can create appendicitis.

The surgeon identifies the triangle of pain, located lateral to the triangle of doom, highlighting the shiny white area where the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve (LFCN) and genitofemoral nerve (GFN) are visible.
GFN is supplying the mouse pubis area in the female and skin of the scrotum in the male and the LFCN is applying the lateral

The surgeon examines the aspect of the upper thigh, identifying a direct hernia located between the inferior epigastric vessel and the medial umbilical ligament. The hernia appears as a small, recurrent black hole. The bladder is visible and slightly distended, while the left side shows no signs of hernia upon thorough examination.

On the left side, the surgeon identifies all ligaments and places an additional port lateral to the inferior epigastric vessel to avoid interference with other instruments. The setup includes two arcs, one 18 cm and the other 24 cm, positioned 6 cm lateral to the outer margin of the defect at the two o'clock position.

The surgeon begins creating the pre-peritoneal pocket by stretching the peritoneum inferiorly and medially. Using harmonic scissors, the peritoneum is cut, and a small nick is made to facilitate further dissection.

The surgeon cuts the peritoneum, allowing gas to enter the pre-peritoneal space. The lower edge of the punctured peritoneum is grasped, and the surgeon uses the tip of the harmonic scalpel or scissors to extend the incision towards the medial umbilical ligament, while stretching the peritoneum inferior medially with one hand.

The surgeon carefully stretches the inferior medial peritoneum to avoid injuring the inferior epigastric vessel. The medial umbilical ligament is visible, and the surgeon begins creating the lateral pocket.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection followed by medial dissection using a blunt instrument, preparing the area for sac dissection in the hernia repair procedure.

The surgeon uses two small atraumatic graspers to push the peritoneum inferomedially with one hand while simultaneously pushing the transversalis fascia away from the peritoneum with the other hand. The dissection is performed carefully to avoid bleeding or creating a buttonhole in the peritoneum, focusing on the area of the triangle of pain.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection, progressing slowly until the spermatic vessels are visible, indicating completion of this dissection phase in a male patient.

The surgeon identifies the testicular vessel, noting its position as the medial boundary of the triangle of pain and the lateral boundary of the triangle of doom, while working within the lateral pocket.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection in the surgical area, using two instruments ambidextrously to carefully manipulate tissues without applying energy, to avoid potential injury.
The anterior peritoneum is visible, and the arc of the transversus abdominis muscle is exposed. The surgeon carefully separates the peritoneum using a laparoscopic approach.

The surgeon completes the lateral dissection and prepares for medial dissection by stretching the medial umbilical ligament. The mesh size is noted to be twelve plus the defect size, with its center aligned to the defect, and minimal fixation is preferred.

The surgeon moves inferomedially, hooking into the groove of the space of the radius. The space of the radius is identified, and the surgeon proceeds to locate the 'lighthouse,' which is the Cooper's ligament. A hernia sac is visible in front of the Cooper's ligament.

The surgeon avoids manipulating the hernia sac initially, focusing instead on medial dissection to clearly define the sac's boundary. Using gentle pressure, the surgeon gradually separates tissue to locate the Cooper's ligament, referred to as the 'lighthouse' in the procedure.
The shiny Cooper ligament becomes visible as the surgeon carefully manipulates the hernia sac, which partially covers the area in cases of direct hernia.

The Cooper ligament, identified as the shiny 'lighthouse,' is visible. The surgeon proceeds with dissection into the space of the radius and continues towards the opposite side, focusing on the medial umbilical ligament. The mesh placement may cross the midline, necessitating further medial dissection.
The surgeon discusses the classification of inguinal hernias, focusing on the medial inguinal hernia, also known as direct hernia, while visually examining the lateral umbilical ligament.
This was given by Professor Rosseri and this is also in the Sages guideline or if you put in the Sages they have described it that lateral medial and median.
The surgeon performs sac dissection, carefully separating the peritoneum of the hernia sac from the transversalis fascia, progressing slowly to ensure clear separation.

The surgeon identifies the neck of the hernia sac, revealing the shiny, pearly white transversalis fascia. The fascia is visible alongside surrounding fat tissue. The hernia sac is clearly seen and is prepared for removal.

The surgeon carefully pulls the peritoneum away from the transverse fascia, separating the sac from the pseudosac. The pseudosac is pushed back into the defect, while the sac, along with any associated lipoma, is extracted.
The surgeon has removed the hernia sac, leaving the shiny pearly white transverse fascia visible. The transverse fascia is carefully pushed back into the defect, completing the extraction of the sac.
The surgeon everts the hernia sac, repositioning and pulling it out, resembling an everted pocket. The sac is now fully exposed and visible.
The surgeon exposes the cupid ligament, making it more clearly visible. Below the ligament, the tendinous arc of the levator ani muscle is observed.

The obturator nerve is visualized near the bladder, within the space of Retzius. The bladder area is clearly visible, and the entire sac has been removed. The surgeon observes the Cooper's ligament on the opposite side, along with the clearly visible pubic symphysis bone.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection to enlarge the pocket, ensuring adequate space for mesh placement. This step is crucial for complete peritonization and preventing mesh crumpling.

And this is the trick of the tap transabdominal preperitoneal hernia surgery. Otherwise you should do IPOM as you know we already do IPOM for those cases where we just take the content out and we cover it and then close it. But here this is now everything is fine.

The surgeon displays the Cooper's ligament, inferior epigastric vessels, triangle of doom, triangle of pain, and trapezoid of disaster, indicating the completion of the pocket formation in the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon identifies the inferior epigastric vessel and the lateral transversus abdominis muscle. A simple proline mesh is prepared for placement in the surgical field.

The surgeon folds the mesh into a plate-like shape and uses a grasper to hold it obliquely, ensuring the tip of the grasper remains outside. The mesh is then carefully pushed into the cannula, with the valve opened to facilitate its insertion.

The surgeon opens a 15 by 12 cm mesh and uses two graspers to spread it within the pocket, ensuring the inferomedial end covers the Cooper ligament. The mesh is carefully positioned to center over the defect, potentially extending slightly to the opposite side.

The surgeon adjusts the mesh placement, ensuring the center of the mesh aligns with the center of the hernia defect. The medial edge of the mesh is pulled up to maintain proper positioning, potentially extending slightly to the opposite side.

The surgeon spreads the mesh evenly over the surgical field, ensuring coverage of the triangle of doom, triangle of pain, and trapezoid of disaster. The mesh is positioned more medially for indirect hernia and slightly lateral for indirect hernia.

The surgeon positions the center of the mesh over the center of the defect, ensuring proper alignment to account for potential shrinkage due to fibrosis. A surgical tacker is then used to secure the mesh medially.

The surgeon positions the mesh over the Cooper ligament and spreads it laterally. The mesh is fixed above the inguinal ligament over the arc of the transversus abdominis muscle, avoiding over-fixation to prevent mesh migration. The fixation is performed just lateral to the inferior epigastric vessel, with another fixation point applied.
The surgeon confirms that the center of the mesh is aligned with the center of the defect. A fixation is applied at the lateral aspect of the Cooper's ligament, completing the procedure.

The surgeon prepares to perform continuous suturing using the Dundee jamming knot technique, with plans for Aberdeen termination. The peritoneum is pulled taut, and the suturing process begins to secure the mesh in place.

Aberdeen termination was invented in University of Aberdeen. And that is also a part of the continuous suturing. So in dandy jamming knot your advantage is just you take a bite on the anterior peritoneum and posterior peritoneum and pass it through the loop and automatically it get locked.

The surgeon takes a bite on the anterior peritoneum and another on the posterior peritoneum over the medial umbilical ligament. The needle is passed through the loop, automatically applying the knot without needing to tie a traditional knot.
The surgeon passes the suture through the loop, demonstrating the self-sliding and self-locking mechanism as the suture is pulled tight.

Nowadays you know in the strata fix suture or V lock at the tail end they make one loop and they do it but they do it now but we are using this knot for last twenty five year and this is was when I was in. Nine wheels at that time only we have been taught to use this knot and it's beautiful.
The surgeon performs continuous suturing, taking a bite on the anterior peritoneum and aligning both edges. The suture is kept slightly loose without being fully tightened.
Do you know why? Because we have a lot of time later to do it. So this is medial end and then you can keep on taking continuous and at the end you will pull it laterally all will be fixed.
The surgeon takes a bite on the upper edge of the peritoneum followed by the lower edge, ensuring the suture remains slightly loose to allow for automatic adjustment.
The surgeon observes the hernia sac forming a pouch within the defect area. The sac, initially positioned in the defect, is visible as the surgical process continues.

You know, peritoneum is a very intelligent structure God has made in our body and it has a great capacity of transmigration and metamorphosis if petronium will get immediately it will get shrink and it will get a become flat within few days you don't need to think about that petronium as we know that it can regenerate also it can reshrink also and if it is loose it will automatically get stretched or shrinked it is up to up to the structure and it is very intelligent also it knows where to cover where not to cover

And where to regenerate, how much to regenerate. So you don't worry about. And some people they fix it. Of course you can do that but not necessary. Especially for a small size of the sack it is not necessary.
The surgeon performs continuous suturing using an endoske needle, pressing the upper jaw of the needle against the bowel surface.
The surgeon performs continuous suturing on the hernia sac, taking bites at approximately two-centimeter intervals. The sutures are kept loose, allowing for adjustment later.

The surgeon takes a bite with the suture needle on the lateral edge, followed by a deeper bite in the undissected virgin area. The needle is then pulled out from the healthy tissue, and the suture is drawn laterally towards the upper abdomen in the same plane.

The surgeon pulls the peritoneum parallel to the abdominal wall, ensuring it closes without cutting through. The insufflation pressure is decreased to eight millimeters of mercury to maintain optimal conditions.
The surgeon completes the peritilization process, ensuring no part of the mesh is visible. They perform aberrant termination by creating a loop within a loop and pulling the needle out.

The surgeon performs Aberdeen termination by creating a loop within a loop, pulling it to lock the suture, and then pulling the non-needle end to secure it further. The needle is then removed, completing the closure of the peritoneum.

The surgeon completes the closure of the peritoneum using sutures, followed by cutting the suture with scissors. The entire peritoneum is visibly closed, and the needle is removed, marking the completion of the transabdominal preperitoneal hernia repair in a female patient.
